metadata.title: Preparation of fibrous polymer grafted magnetic chitosan beads and modified with hydrazone and imidazole groups: isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater
metadata.abstract: Abstract The magnetic chitosan beads were prepared and grafted with glycidylmethacrylate, then diaminomelanonitrile (DAMN) was attached. The nitrile groups of DAMN were reacted with hydrazine hydrate hydrochloride and hydrazine hydrate-ethylene diamine to obtain hydrazine (HYD) and imidazole (IMDZ) groups, respectively. Both adsorbents removed uranyl ions from the aqueous medium and seawater. The adsorption capacities of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ, beads were 328.4 and 434.7 mg/g, respectively. Both adsorbents have high performance for adsorbing U(VI) ions from seawater. The synthesized adsorbents were used to isolate U(VI) ions from seawater in the presence of various ions.
metadata.journal: Journal of Radioanalytical and Nuclear Chemistry

sections.heading: Preparation of fibrous polymer grafted magnetic chitosan beads and modified with hydrazone and imidazole groups: isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater
sections.content:
Serhad Tilki1,2 ¬∑ Omur CelikbńĪcak3 ¬∑ Ilkay AcńĪkgoz‚ÄĎErkaya4 ¬∑ Mehmet Yakup Arica1 ¬∑ Gulay Bayramoglu1,5

Received: 7 December 2024 / Accepted: 2 March 2025 / Published online: 29 March 2025 ¬© The Author(s) 2025

subsections.heading: Abstract
subsections.content:
The magnetic chitosan beads were prepared and grafted with glycidylmethacrylate, then diaminomelanonitrile (DAMN) was attached. The nitrile groups of DAMN were reacted with hydrazine hydrate hydrochloride and hydrazine hydrateethylene diamine to obtain hydrazine (HYD) and imidazole (IMDZ) groups, respectively. Both adsorbents removed uranyl ions from the aqueous medium and seawater. The adsorption capacities of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ, beads were 328.4 and 434.7 mg/g, respectively. Both adsorbents have high performance for adsorbing U(VI) ions from seawater. The synthesized adsorbents were used to isolate U(VI) ions from seawater in the presence of various ions.

Keywords‚Äā Chitosan-based adsorbent ¬∑ Surface modification ¬∑ Adsorption ¬∑ Uranium ¬∑ Isotherms and Kinetics

subsections.heading: Introduction
subsections.content:
Developing clean and renewable energy has become one of the global concerns [1‚Äď4], and long-term sustainable global energy supply has become one of the most important problems for humanity [5, 6]. The present energy resources, such as petrol, oil, natural gas, and mineral gases, are restricted and can be run out. Therefore, with the rapid increase in global energy demands, nuclear power has appeared as a viable solution to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions and increase energy intensity [7‚Äď9]. In the nuclear industry,

U(VI) is a crucial element widely distributed in the Earth‚Äôs crust and especially abundant in seawater. The known reserves of uranium ore on land can be inadequate to meet the future demands of nuclear power plants. The estimated amounts of uranium in the seas are approximately 4.5 billion tons, around a thousand times more than the total land-based reserves [10, 11]. Therefore, selective and easy isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater is essential for the sustainable supply of uranium to nuclear energy resources [10‚Äď12]. Many methods have been reported for the isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater, such as chemical precipitation, photo-catalysis, membrane filtration, and adsorption [13‚Äď17]. Among these techniques, adsorption can be considered a sustainable method for U(VI) removal from seawater due to its low cost, large-scale applicability, high efficacy, and easy operation [18‚Äď22]. Numerous types of sorbents have been used for the adsorption of inorganic and organic from environment, including active carbon [23], composite materials [24, 25], cellulose [17, 26], chitosan [27, 28], metal‚Äďorganic framework [29], layered double zinc and aluminum hydroxide [30‚Äď32], hydrogels [33], various polymeric materials [34‚Äď36], biosorbents [24, 37], and magnetic adsorbents [38‚Äď41]. However, these prepared adsorbents have some advantages and disadvantages, and thus, to prepare reliable adsorbents with desired properties that are costeffective, easy to produce, highly durable, and possess a high

adsorption capacity under various experimental conditions. It should be noted that adsorbents containing amine groups are important for rapid adsorption because they easily interact with metal ions. Thus, they could effectively remove uranyl ions from seawater [7, 33, 38, 42‚Äď44]. In previous studies, researchers preferred to develop structures containing amine groups, especially polyamine and imidazole groups, when designing new adsorbents. For example, Bayramoglu et al. prepared agmatine ligand-modified magnetic alginate beads, and the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for U(VI) was ions 451.4 mg/g. Bayramoglu and Arica synthesized poly amidoxime group functionalized magnetic adsorbent and removed U(VI) ions from aqueous solutions. The maximum adsorption pH was observed at 6.0. Yang et al. [27] prepared amidoxime-modified chitosan adsorbent, uranium removal was studied from aqueous solution and seawater, and the maximum uranium adsorption capacities were 596 mg/g, and 243.2 őľg/g, respectively. Ivanov et al. (2024) prepared ¬≠Fe3O4 coated by Zn and Al layered double hydroxide for U(VI) removal, and the adsorption exchange capacity towards U(VI) ions was reported as 268.65 mg/g [32]. Additionally, adsorbents should be easily separable from the adsorption medium and not generate secondary pollutants. Magnetic materials can be simply removed from the solutions by using an external magnet. For this reason, magnetic adsorbents have received significant attention for removing metal ions from solutions [13, 32, 38]. A short time since nanotechnology has been applied in all branches of the sciences. With this understanding, new-generation adsorbents were prepared by grafting comb-type polymer chains in adsorption technology. In this concept, magnetic composite material can be grafted with a comb-type polymer to improve its adsorption performance using different functional polymers. Among the grafting methods, redox polymerization grafting is an easy polymerization method [35], which does not involve severe experimental conditions. In the grafting polymerization method, GMA is a widely used monomer, and its polymer has reactive epoxy groups. It can be utilized for coupling reactions with amine and thiol groups of the ligand molecules. Furthermore, the graft polymerization method generates fibrous polymer on the magnetic adsorbent, and various ligands can be attached to the fibrous polymer. This kind of adsorbent offers a high surface area [1]. In this study, magnetic chitosan beads were prepared via the phase inversion method and functionalized with hydrazone and imidazole groups. The synthesized adsorbents were characterized by FT-IR, SEM, BET, and analytical methods. The adsorption capacities of both adsorbents (i.e., Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads) for uranium under various experimental conditions were investigated in batch and continuous systems. The kinetic and thermodynamic models evaluated the adsorption mechanism between U(VI)

ions and the adsorbents. Finally, the performance of the assynthesized adsorbent was tested for adsorption of U(VI) ions from seawater obtained from the Black Sea and the Mediterranean Sea.

subsections.heading: Materials and methods
subsections.content:
Materials

Glycidyl methacrylate (GA), diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN, 98%), ethylenediamine, hydrazine hydroxylamine ¬≠(NH2OH), and chitosan (high molecular weight, deacetylation‚ÄČ‚Č•‚ÄČ75%), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Hamburg, Germany). U(VI) solution was prepared by dissolving uranyl acetate ¬≠(UO2(CH3COO)2¬∑2H2O) in deionized water and supplied from Honeywell Riedel-de-Haen AG (Germany). ¬≠FeCl3, ¬≠FeSO4¬∑7H2O, isopropanol (IPA), and all other chemicals were of analytical grade and purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

subsections.heading: Preparation of magnetic nanoparticles
subsections.content: The magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized via thermal co-precipitation reaction of Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions in a 2:1 molar ratio. For this purpose, ¬≠FeCl3 (6.5 g, 0.04 mol) and ¬≠FeSO4¬∑7H2O (5.6 g, 0.02 mol) were separately dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water. Then, the ¬≠FeCl3 (50 mL) and ¬≠FeSO4 (50 mL) solutions were transferred to a reaction vessel, and the temperature was raised to 45 ¬įC. After reaching the thermal equilibrium, ammonia solution (50 mL, 25% w/v) was added dropwise. The reaction temperature was set to 75 ¬įC and allowed to proceed for two hours under a nitrogen atmosphere. After this period, the reaction temperature was raised to 85 ¬įC and continued for one hour. After the reaction period, the magnetic nanoparticles ¬≠(Fe3O4) were separated using an external magnet and washed several times with distilled water to remove impurities. Then, they were dried in a vacuum oven under reduced pressures at 45 ¬įC.

subsections.heading: Synthesis of magnetic chitosan beads
subsections.content: Magnetic chitosan beads were synthesized via the phase inversion method, as described previously [28]. Briefly, chitosan solution was prepared by adding 0.4 g of chitosan flake in 10% acetic acid solution (v/v, 40 mL). Then, 1.0 g of the above magnetic nanoparticles were added and magnetically mixed until a homogeneous solution was obtained. This mixture was dropped into NaOH solution (2.5 mol/L) using a syringe pump through an injection needle while continuously stirring the solution. The resulting ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT spheres were washed with distilled water and stored.

subsections.heading: Grafting of magnetic chitosan beads with GMA and modification into hydrazone and imidazole groups
subsections.content: The magnetic chitosan beads were grafted with glycidylmethacrylate. The grafting reaction time was realized at different reaction times between 3.0 and 10 h to obtain the desired amount of GMA grafting density. Briefly, the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads (~‚ÄČ300 mg) were transferred to a reaction vessel, then isopropanol (20 mL) and deionized water (20 mL) were added and heated to 50 ¬įC under a nitrogen atmosphere. For the grafting reaction, GMA (2.0 mL) and KMnO‚āĄ (20 mL, 2.0 mmol/L) were added to this mixture, and the reaction was continued for 10 h. After this period, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads were washed with distilled water and ethanol 70% (v/v) to remove reaction impurities and then stored in ethanol (70%, v/v). Diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN) ligand was covalently attached to the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads via the reaction between the epoxy groups of p(GMA) and the amino groups of DAMN. Briefly, DAMN ligand (0.3 g) was dissolved in ethanol (100 mL, pH 9.0), and ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA) beads 0.5 g were added. The reaction mixture was rotated at 150 rpm and 60 ¬įC for 12 h. After the reaction, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN beads were washed with ethanol (70%, v/v) and distilled water and stored at room temperature. For hydrazone group modification, ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN beads (0.5) were transferred into a reaction medium containing hydrazine hydrate (15 mL) and ethanol (5 mL) at pH 8.0. The reaction medium was refluxed at 85 ¬įC for 6.0 h. At the end of the reaction period, the hydrazine-modified beads (i.e., ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD) were cleaned with ethanol and distilled water and then stored at room temperature. For imidazole group generation on the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD beads were reacted with ethylene diamine (EDA). An EDA solution (33%, 80 mL) containing 0.1 g of sulfur was prepared for this. Then, the solution temperature was raised to 90 ¬įC, and the dried ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA-HYD beads were added to the reaction medium. The reaction was continued at 95 ¬įC‚ÄČ¬Ī‚ÄČ5 ¬įC for 7.0 h. After the reaction period, the imidazole-modified beads ¬≠(Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ) were washed with deionized water at 50 ¬įC and then stored in ethanol: water mixture (70:30, v/v) at room temperature.

subsections.heading: Characterization studies
subsections.content:
The ATR-FTIR spectra of magnetic ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ, and beads were obtained using an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet iS5, Thermo Fisher Scientific, U.S.A.). The

surface morphology of the as-prepared magnetic beads was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI QUANTA 250 FEG, Oregon, USA). The magnetic properties of the ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, and ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA) beads were studied using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, 880; ADE Technologies, USA). The surface areas and average pore sizes of the ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads were obtained by the Brunauer‚ÄďEmmett‚ÄďTeller (BET) analysis. The ¬≠N2 adsorption/desorption experiments were studied using ASAP2020 HD88. The amount of epoxy groups on the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads was determined using the pyridine-HCl method described previously [1]. An ICP-MS spectrometer Thermo Fisher (iCAP TQe00061, Scientific, USA) with a plasma generator and the power output 1550 was used to determine metals. The as-prepared materials‚Äô x-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using Cu-KőĪ radiation, between 2őł of 20 and 80¬į, with a 0.1 increment and 2¬į ¬≠min‚ąí1 scan speed (MiniFlex 600, Rigaku).

subsections.heading: Adsorption studies
subsections.content:
The adsorption performances of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ were studied batch-wise. The adsorption experiments were conducted in a vial containing 20 mg of adsorbents and 30 mL of uranyl ions solution. The vials were placed on a shaker and rotated at 200 rpm and 25‚ÄČ¬Ī‚ÄČ2 ¬įC. The influence of pH on the adsorption performance was examined between pH 3.0 and 8.0, and U(VI) ions concentration was 200 mg/L. The effect of adsorbent amount on the adsorption performance was studied with different amounts of adsorbent in the range of 0.2‚Äď1.0 g/L with a uranium ions concentration of 200 mg/L. The impact of the initial concentration of uranium ions on adsorption capacity was investigated by varying the concentration of U(VI) ions between 1.0 and 400.0 mg/L. The effect of contact time on adsorption was investigated at a concentration of 400 mg/L of uranium ions for 180 min, and the sample was taken at predetermined time intervals. The adsorption performance of the adsorbents was also studied in synthetic seawater. One liter of synthetic seawater comprised of the following ingredients: NaCl: 24.53 g, MgCl‚āā: 5.20 g, CaCl‚āā:1.16 g, KCl: 0.695 g, ¬≠CuCl2: 20 mg, ¬≠NiCl2: 20 mg and ¬≠PbCl2 20 mg) and various amount of uranium ions was added to this solution between 0.5 and 10.0 mg/L. The amount of adsorbed U(VI) ions per unit of adsorbent [mg U(VI)/g adsorbent] was calculated by using the following equation:

(1) qe = (C0 ‚ąíCe‚ąēm) V

where ¬≠qe is the amount of U(VI) ion adsorbed onto the unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g), ¬≠C0 and ¬≠Ce are the concentrations

of the U(VI) ion before and after adsorption (mg/L), V is the volume of the solution (L), and m is the amount of the adsorbent (g).

subsections.heading: Competing adsorption studies from binary mixture
subsections.content: The selectivity of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads towards U(VI) ions (ionic radius: 138 pm) in the presence of competitive ions Cu(II) (ionic radius: 71 pm) and/or Ni(II) (ionic radius: 73 pm) was evaluated in a batch system. The metal ions solutions were prepared from Cu(NO3)2¬∑3H2O, Ni(NO3)2¬∑6H2O and Pb(NO3)2. A solution (20 mL) containing 1.0 mmol/L U(VI) ions and Cu(II) ions, Ni(II) or Pb(II) ions each of them 20 mmol/L was contacted with 1.0 g/L of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads at pH 6.0 and 25 ¬įC in the vial stirred at 200 rpm. After the adsorption period, the remaining concentration of each metal ion in the test solution was determined, as described above.

subsections.heading: Removal of U(VI) ions from seawater in the continuous system
subsections.content:
Removal of U(VI) ions from seawater in a continuous system was studied with Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads. The adsorbent was packed in a packed bed reactor (length: 14 cm; radius: 0.8 cm; volume: 28 mL), and the reactor was fed separately with the Black Sea water and the Mediterranean Sea water samples from the bottom inlet part at a flow rate of 100 mL/h with a peristaltic pump (model

IPC, Ismatec, D√ľsseldorf, Germany). The reactor was packed approximately with 3.8 g adsorbent and operated at room temperature (approximately 25 ¬įC) for 24 h.

subsections.heading: Desorption studies
subsections.content:
Adsorbed U(VI) ions were desorbed from the adsorbent by using 0.1 mol/L nitric acid solution. The Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were located in the desorption medium and stirred at 200 rpm at room temperature for 2.0 h. To determine the reusability of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads successive adsorption/ desorption rounds were repeated five times by using the same adsorbents. The desorption ratio was calculated from the amount of U(VI) ions released by the adsorbent and the final concentration of U(VI) ions in the adsorption medium.

Results and discussion

subsections.heading: Characterization studies
subsections.content:
A two-step process involved preparing ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads. ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads were prepared from magnetic nanoparticles and chitosan via the phase inversion method in the first step. The second step consisted of the graft polymerization of GMA monomer via redox polymerization in the presence of KMnO‚āĄ. The schematic representation of the preparation of adsorbents is presented in Fig. 1. The grafting

Fig.‚ÄĮ1‚Äā ‚ÄČSchematic representation of preparations of hydrazide and imidazole-modified adsorbents

reaction time was optimized to obtain high adsorption performance towards uranyl ions, and the polymerization time was realized up to 10 h. The amount of grafted p(GMA) on the magnetic chitosan beads was detected by the pyridineHCl method. The functional epoxy group content of the beads was found to be between 0.63 and 1.87 mmol/g beads. The ATR-FTIR spectra of ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMZD beads are presented in Fig. 2. The characteristic peak at 599 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 corresponded to the Fe‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČO stretching vibration of iron oxide nanoparticles. The presence of this peak confirmed the successful synthesis of ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 2A). The IR spectrum of chitosan exhibited characteristic peaks at 3354 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 due to‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČNH and ‚ÄďOH stretching vibrations. The peak at 1578 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 could have resulted from the ‚ÄďNH2 stretching vibration of chitosan (Fig. 2B). The FTIR spectra were also obtained to confirm the presence of ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the chitosan beads at 599 ¬≠cm‚ąí1. The

Fig.‚ÄĮ2‚Äā ‚ÄČFT-IR Spectra of A ¬≠Fe3O4, B ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, C ¬≠Fe3O4@CHTg-p(GMA), D ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-DAMN, E ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-gp(GMA)-HYD, and F ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-IMDZ

IR spectrum of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT displayed a band at 3347 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 due to‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČNH or ‚ÄďOH stretching vibrations. Similar peaks (namely amide I, amid II, and Fe‚ÄďO were observed in this spectrum as in the case of pure chitosan. The FT-IR spectra of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads with characteristic peaks of epoxy and carbonyl groups at 901 cm‚ĀĽ1 and at 1717 cm‚ĀĽ1 were observed, respectively (Fig. 2C). The FTIR spectrum for ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN is presented in Fig. 2D. As observed from this spectrum, the band is due to antisymmetric stretching vibrations of the ¬≠NH2 groups observed at 3384 ¬≠cm‚ąí1. The characteristic stretching vibration peak of C‚Č°N was observed at 2360 ¬≠cm‚ąí1. The band at 1716 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 could be assigned for the ¬≠NH2 scissoring vibrations. The FT-IR spectra of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD beads showed peaks at around 3344 cm‚ĀĽ1 and 1640 cm‚ĀĽ1, corresponding to ‚ÄďNH‚āā stretching vibration and N‚ÄďH deformation, respectively, indicating the incorporation of amino groups from hydrazine into the structure of nitrile groups of DAMN on the beads (Fig. 3E). The FT-IR spectra of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ exhibited new bands at 3311 cm‚ĀĽ1 for N‚ÄďH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1528 and 1385 cm‚ĀĽ1 could be assigned for ‚ÄďNH2 and ‚ÄďCN stretching vibrations, confirming the formation of imidazole groups onto the HYD-modified beads after reaction with ethylene diamine (Fig. 2F). Furthermore, the new peak observed at 918 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 is related to the waging of the primary and secondary amines of the imidazole ring. Thus, as observed changes in the spectra, we conclude that the attached DAMN ligands were successfully converted to hydrazone and imidazole groups on the magnetic beads. The SEM image can provide information on the size, shape, and surface morphology of the as-prepared adsorbent material. The surface morphologies of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA) beads were studied using scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 3). As observed in Fig. 3A, a sphere-like structure was obtained for ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads, and at high magnification, the surface of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads was found to be a highly irregular structure (Fig. 3B). After grafting with p(GMA), a structural change in the surface morphology of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads was observed and suggested that the grafting of the surface of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads with p(GMA) was successfully achieved (Fig. 3C). The surface of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads was highly pores. Thus, the porous surface can provide a large surface area, and as a result, the overall surface area can be significantly enhanced. Grafting with p(GMA) may increase the contact surface area and adsorption capacity of uranium ions in the solution. The magnetic properties of the as-prepared materials were obtained using a VSM. As shown in Fig. 4, the magnetic hysteresis curves of the ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) beads were attained S-shaped over the elaborated magnetic field, representing

Fig.‚ÄĮ3‚Äā ‚ÄČSEM images of a ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads at‚ÄČ~‚ÄČ160X, b ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads at‚ÄČ~‚ÄČ4kX, c ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads at‚ÄČ~‚ÄČ4kX

Fig.‚ÄĮ4‚Äā ‚ÄČVSM profiles of ¬≠Fe3O4, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-DAMN (A); XRD patterns of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT-g-p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-IMDZ (B)

that the tested materials had magnetic properties (Fig. 4). ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHTg-p(GMA) beads revealed durable magnetism with low remanence and coercivity, indicating these magnetic materials were easily removed from the adsorption medium by just placing in a magnetic field after laden with pollutants. The magnetic performances of the ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles,

¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA), and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHTg-p(GMA)-DAMN beads were found to be 41.3, 32.7, 18.9 and 18.4 emu/g, respectively. The magnetic properties of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) decreased compared to ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the amount of nonmagnetic materials increased. The modification of the DAMN ligand into hydrazone and imidazole groups was

not caused any change in the magnetic properties of the adsorbents. The XRD patterns of XRD patterns of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHTg-p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT-g-p(GMA)-IMDZ are presented in Fig. 7B. From this figure, diffraction peaks for the polymers grafted magnetic particles were detected at around 2őł‚ÄČ=‚ÄČ20¬į. The observed peaks in the XRD pattern of the as-prepared materials were pertinent to the crystalline phase of the beads. By matching with the reference peaks of the magnetic materials, six diffraction peaks around 2őł‚ÄČ=‚ÄČ32.7¬į, 36.8¬į, 43.5¬į, 47.2¬į, 58.3¬į, and 64.8¬į were associated with the facecentered cubic structure of magnetic particles within the bead structure. The reflection peaks agreed well with the X-ray diffraction data cards (ICDD number 88‚Äď0315) with six diffraction peaks: 220, 311, 400, 422, 511, and 440, respectively. These reflection peaks agree well with the X-ray diffraction data cards (ICDD number 88‚Äď0315) with six diffraction peaks: 220, 311, 400, 422, 511, and 440, respectively. As expected, the magnetic properties of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) was not affected by the modification reactions as observed from XRD data. The average bead size distribution of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) was found to be 1.1‚ÄČ¬Ī‚ÄČ0.2 and 1.3‚ÄČ¬Ī‚ÄČ0.3 mm, respectively. The DAMN ligand attachment and modification reactions were not significantly affected the beads size distribution. The measurements of surface area and pore size distributions of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT-g-p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-DAMN, ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT-g-p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)IMDZ beads samples were studied low-temperature nitrogen adsorption‚Äďdesorption isotherms. The surface areas of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) beads were obtained with nitrogen adsorption/desorption experiments. The surface area of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT was 1.93 g/m2. On the other hand, the surface area of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) beads was 5.96 g/m2, and the area was increased by about three folds after grafting with p(GMA), the average pore size of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) was 1.85 ¬Ķm. Thus, a high surface area and porous surface were generated on the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads via grafting with p(GMA). The results showed that the surface area of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) beads considerably increased after grafting with p(GMA) polymer. The surface area of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)DAMN, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHTg-p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were found to be 4.71, 4.18 and 3.94 mg/g beads, and average pore sizes were 1.78, 1.66 and 1.48 mm, respectively. The surface areas and average pore sizes of the DAMN ligand attached and modified beads decreased compared to bare ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA) beads; these decreases might be due to the accumulation of ligand molecules on and around the surface of the pores, eventually leading to the blockage of the pore mouth.

subsections.heading: Effect of adsorbent dose on adsorption efficiency of U(VI) ions
subsections.content: The amount of adsorbent in the medium is a significant factor in pollutant removal studies. The adsorption of U(VI) onto Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ beads was studied at different amounts of adsorbent dose and changed between 0.2 and 1.0 g/L with an initial concentration of 200 mg/L. The adsorption efficiency of adsorbents for U(VI) under different adsorbent amounts is presented in Fig. 5. As seen in this figure, the amount of adsorbed U(VI) progressively increased with the increase of the adsorbent dosage. When the amount of adsorbent was 0.6 g/L, the removal percentage of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads reached 96.7% and 98.9%, respectively. Then, the removal percentage did not enhance meaningfully with increasing the adsorbent dosage. This could be because the available binding sites on the increasing amount of adsorbent could not be saturated with constant uranyl ions concentration in the solution. Thus, the amount of adsorbed U(VI) ions by each tested adsorbent was sensible with the residual amount of uranyl ions in the solution, causing a quiet adsorption percentage. As the adsorbent dosage was enhanced, the amount of adsorbed uranyl ions on the unit of the adsorbent dose gradually reduced due to a decrease in the amount of U(VI) ions in the unit adsorbent mass. For the best results in the remaining studies, 0.6 g/L of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were used in the U(VI) ions adsorption test.

subsections.heading: Effect of pH on adsorption performance of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)‚ÄĎHYD or Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)‚ÄĎIMDZ beads
subsections.content:
The pH value of the aqueous solution is crucial in regulating the uranium species and the surface properties of the adsorbent, thereby influencing the uranium adsorption capacity [9]. Studies on the effect of pH on the adsorption of U(VI) ions onto Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were carried out in the pH range of 3.0‚Äď8.0. Moreover, control experiments without adsorbent were realized, and no precipitation of uranyl ions on the vial walls and any surface was observed at the studied pH range. The maximum uranyl ions adsorption on the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads was observed between pH 6.0 and 7.0 (Fig. 6A). As observed from this figure, as the solution pH increased, the adsorption capacities of the tested adsorbents increased to pH 7.0 and then decreased. The protonation of hydrazone and imidazole groups of both adsorbents resulted in a positive surface charge on both adsorbents under acidic conditions. The pKa values of

Fig.‚ÄĮ5‚Äā ‚ÄČAdsorption performance

of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads for U(VI) ions. (U(VI) concentration: 200 mg/L, Volume: 20 mL, pH: 6.0, Temperature: 25 ¬įC, Stirring rate: 200 rpm)

the hydrazone and imidazole groups of the adsorbents are 7.0 and 10.2, respectively. Thus, at the studied pH range, both adsorbent‚Äôs surface is positively charged [45]. Thus, the surface of both adsorbents is protonated and positively charged at the studied pH range. At pH‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČ5.0, uranyl ions species are positively charged predominantly in the form of ¬≠UO2 2+; thus, the electrostatic repulsion of both adsorbate and binding sites could prevent U(VI) ions from approaching the active binding center of the adsorbent, resulting in a decrease in the adsorption capacity of both adsorbent toward U(VI) ions. Whereas at pH‚ÄČ‚Č•‚ÄČ5.5, negatively charged uranyl ionic species such as ¬≠UO2(CO3)2 2‚ąí, ¬≠UO2(CO3)3 4‚ąí, ¬≠UO2(OH)3‚ąí, (UO‚āā)‚āÉ(OH)10 4‚ĀĽand ¬≠(UO2)3(OH)7‚ąí can be formed with increasing pH value of the medium. Therefore, the amount of adsorbed uranyl ions on the adsorbents can depend on the distribution of uranyl ion species at given pH values in the medium [46‚Äď49]. As a result, the adsorption capacity of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads for uranyl ions were observed maximum at between pH 6.0 and 7.0. Due to the strong complexation of uranyl ions with the given above uranyl species, the main uranyl species were ¬≠UO2(CO3)2 2‚ąí and ¬≠UO2(CO3)3 4‚ąí at around pH 6.0 [1, 44]. Thus, the observed maximum adsorption capacities of uranyl ions on Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@

CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads between pH 6.0 and 7.0 could be explained by the affirmative coordination conditions between uranyl ionic species hydrazine and imidazole groups. The uranium (VI) speciation diagram is also presented in Fig. 6B. As can be seen from the figure, the significant forms of uranyl ions between pH 6.0 and 7.0 are ¬≠UO2CO3, ¬≠(UO2)CO3(OH)3 ‚ąí and ¬≠UO2(CO3)2 2‚ąí [44]. From these observations, the binding interaction of uranium ions on Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads could be considered primarily as coordination complex formation, ion exchange interaction, and physical forces. It should be noted that Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ presented better adsorption capability for U(VI) ions than that of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD.

As seen in this figure, as the initial concentration of U(VI) ions in the medium increased, the adsorption capacities of the tested adsorbents increased. Because the initial concentration generates a driving force to overcome the counteraction of the mass transfer of uranyl ions between the adsorbent and medium [50‚Äď53], as the initial concentration of uranyl ions increases, the driving force increases, and thus, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent will aggrandize. It should be noted that the modification ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN beads into hydrazine and imidazole groups increased the adsorption capacity by about 2.64 and 3.50 fold, respectively, compared to the parent compound.

subsections.content:
Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ beads were used to enrich uranyl ions from synthetic seawater. The former consisted of the following ingredients in g/1.0 L distilled water: NaCl: 24.5, MgCl‚āā 5.20, CaCl‚āā: 1.2, KCl: 0.70, and CuCl, NiCl, and PbCl each 20 mg/L. with varying concentrations of uranium (i.e., 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, and 8.0 mg/L) and spiked with various amounts of uranyl ions. The performance of the tested two adsorbents in the synthetic seawater samples is shown in Fig. 9. The removal efficiencies of U(VI) ions by ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD were 72.1, 66.4, 58.3 and 55.8%, and for ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ were 83.6, 74.2, 68.9, and 61.7%, respectively, in U(VI) ions uranium spiked synthetic seawater with U(VI) concentration of 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, and 8.0 mg/L, after 3.0 h contact period. high removal performance in the samples spiked with varying amounts of uranyl ions. This result indicates that the synthesized adsorbents could be used for the recovery of uranyl ions from complex environment samples such as seawater, which contains various ionic species. The removal of uranyl ions by ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads decreased with increasing spiked amounts of U(VI) ions in the medium, and the percent removal efficiency of U(VI) ions decreased for adsorbents from 72.1 to 55.8% and from 83.6 to 61.7%, respectively. The removal of the uranyl ions from the Black Sea and Mediterranean Sea Waters using ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ were also studied in a batch system, and the removal performance of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD was found to be 62.2 and 18.4%, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ was 87.9 and 24.1%, respectively. It should be remembered that the Black Sea Water has a higher amount of uranium concentration than the Mediterranean Sea Water.

Electrostatic and ion-exchange interactions can elucidate the adsorption mechanism of U(VI) ions by the ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads. The hydrazone groups are the primary functional binding sites of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD adsorbent. On the other hand, the amount of nitrogen content of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads was further increased by incorporating ethylenediamine on the hydrazone structure to get obtain imidazole groups. These amine-rich groups have a high affinity toward metal ions. Both adsorbents adsorbed U(VI) ions with high affinity during adsorption, as observed from the above experimental results. As evidence, EDXS data were obtained from U(VI) ions laden ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads. The SEM micrographs, energy-dispersive-X-ray spectrum (EDXS), and SEM‚ÄďEDX mapping analysis data for ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ beads are presented in Fig. 10 (partly A, B, C and D, E, F, respectively). As can be seen from Fig. 10, the energy dispersive X-ray spectrum and elemental mappings were obtained to evaluate the elemental distribution of adsorbents. As observed from this figure, the uniform distribution of hydrazone and imidazole major functional binding groups on the respective adsorbent resulted in uniform adsorption of uranyl ions on both adsorbent surfaces. Thus, it can be supposed that the data attained were clear evidence of the adsorption of uranyl ions on both adsorbent beads. Furthermore, as given in Fig. 2, the FT-IR spectrum of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD beads displayed peaks at around 3344 cm‚ĀĽ1 and 1640 cm‚ĀĽ1, corresponding to ‚ÄďNH‚āā stretching vibration and N‚ÄďH deformation, respectively, indicating the presence of amine groups. The FT-IR spectrum of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ had a peak at 3311 cm‚ĀĽ1 for N‚ÄďH stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1528 cm‚ĀĽ1 and 1385 cm‚ĀĽ1 were observed for ‚ÄďNH2 and ‚ÄďCN stretching vibrations, confirming the formation of imidazole groups onto the HYD-modified beads after reaction with ethylene diamine. Therefore, these functional groups should be responsible for the electrostatic and ion-exchange

subsections.heading: Competitive metal ions adsorption studies
subsections.content:
To assess the adsorption selectivity of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads adsorption experiments were performed in the binary competitive systems with (U(VI)/Cu(II), U(VI)/ Ni(II), and U(VI)/Pb(II) ions. In the binary and single systems, the adsorption studies of uranyl ions with these metal ions were achieved at pH 6.0 and with a concentration of metal ions 20 mmol/L. Figure 11 shows the amounts of adsorbed metal ions on the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads in the single and binary metal ions systems. As observed from this figure, the presence of Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) reduced the adsorption capacities of the adsorbents for uranyl ions to some extent. The reduction in U(VI) adsorption capacity of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD in the presence of Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) was 8.6, 6.3, and 10.0%, respectively. At the same time, the adsorption capacity of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ for U(VI) was reduced by about 11.7, 28.9 and 25.6% in the presence of co-existence metals (i.e., Cu(II), Ni(II) and Pb(II)), respectively. The reduction in U(VI) adsorption capacity of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD in the presence of Cu(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II) was 8.6, 6.3, and 10.0%, respectively. The adsorption capacity of

subsections.heading: Regeneration and repeated use
subsections.content:
For removing pollutants from wastewater, the ability to regenerate the adsorbent is a crucial economic consideration. Therefore, U(VI) ions laden ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were desorbed with a dilute nitric acid solution ¬≠(HNO3, 50 mM). The desorption series was repeated five times using the same magnetic beads. As can be seen in Fig. 12, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads continued a stable adsorption ratio of approximately 95 and 91%, respectively, and the adsorption efficiencies of adsorbents for uranyl ions were merely decreased by about 5.0 and 9.0%.

subsections.heading: Removal of U(VI) ions from seawater in a continuous system
subsections.content:
Comparison of the prepared Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)‚ÄĎHYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)‚ÄĎIMDZ beads with earlier studies

Many continuous systems have been reported, such as stirred tank reactors, fluidized beds, and packed bed reactors. These continuous systems may exclude additional operational processes, such as filtration and centrifugation that require extra operational costs. Among these systems, the operation of a packed bed reactor may be easiest compared to another reactor system. The removal percentage of U(VI) in continuous systems using the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads was investigated. Determination of the adsorption of U(VI) in a continuous system can be important for understanding the dynamic behavior of the adsorption process. Therefore, the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were operated in a packed bed reactor and used to remove U(VI) ions from the Black Sea and Mediterranean Sea waters. The initial concentrations of U(VI) in the reactor feed solutions were 9.4 and 5.5 pbb for the Black Sea and Mediterranean Sea waters, respectively. After a 24 h continuous operation of the reactor with a sample of sea waters, as described above, the

It is essential to compare the adsorption efficiency of new adsorbents with present adsorbents for environmental applications. From Table 3, the adsorption capacities of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ beads for the uranyl ions can be compared to the reported works in the literature [14, 31, 32, 37, 38, 54‚Äď57]. The observed high adsorption efficiencies of the as-prepared adsorbents can result from electrostatic and coordination complex interactions between the hydrazone and imidazole groups of each adsorbent and U(VI) ions. As presented above, the imidazole groups were more reasonable for removing U(VI) ions from the medium. The high adsorption performance of the imidazole groups for uranyl ions can be a result of the specific interaction of this functional group with uranyl ions.

subsections.heading: Conclusion
subsections.content: In this study, magmatic chitosan beads were prepared via the phase inversion method and functionalized with diaminomaleonitrile ligand. The nitrile groups of the ligand were reacted with hydroxylamine and hydrazine hydrate, and the hydrozane and imidazole groups were obtained on the surface of the adsorbent and called ‚ÄúFe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads, respectively. The prepared adsorbents were characterized using FT-IR, SEM, VSM, and BET methods. The prepared ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were used for uranyl ions recovery from the aqueous medium, synthetic seawater in a batch system, and real seawater from the continuous system. The maximum adsorption capacities of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were determined as 328.4 and 434.7 mg/g at pH 6.0, respectively. After five adsorption/desorption cycles, the uranium adsorption capacity was more than 87% of the initial adsorption capacity. The theoretical U(VI) ions adsorption capacity obtained from the Langmuir isotherm model was very near the experimental U(VI) ions adsorption capacity of the tested adsorbents. According to the tested kinetic models, the adsorption kinetics data of U(VI) ions on both adsorbents fitted the pseudo-second-order kinetics, which proposed that the chemisorption was the rate-limiting step. This research provides valuable insights into the potential of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads for effective removal of trace amount uranyl ions from synthetic seawater and real seawater.

Preparation of fibrous polymer grafted magnetic chitosan beads and modified with hydrazone and imidazole groups: isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater
Serhad Tilki1,2 ¬∑ Omur CelikbńĪcak3 ¬∑ Ilkay AcńĪkgoz‚ÄĎErkaya4 ¬∑ Mehmet Yakup Arica1 ¬∑ Gulay Bayramoglu1,5
Received: 7 December 2024 / Accepted: 2 March 2025 / Published online: 29 March 2025 ¬© The Author(s) 2025
Abstract
The magnetic chitosan beads were prepared and grafted with glycidylmethacrylate, then diaminomelanonitrile (DAMN) was attached. The nitrile groups of DAMN were reacted with hydrazine hydrate hydrochloride and hydrazine hydrateethylene diamine to obtain hydrazine (HYD) and imidazole (IMDZ) groups, respectively. Both adsorbents removed uranyl ions from the aqueous medium and seawater. The adsorption capacities of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ, beads were 328.4 and 434.7 mg/g, respectively. Both adsorbents have high performance for adsorbing U(VI) ions from seawater. The synthesized adsorbents were used to isolate U(VI) ions from seawater in the presence of various ions.
Keywords‚Äā Chitosan-based adsorbent ¬∑ Surface modification ¬∑ Adsorption ¬∑ Uranium ¬∑ Isotherms and Kinetics
Introduction
Developing clean and renewable energy has become one of the global concerns [1‚Äď4], and long-term sustainable global energy supply has become one of the most important problems for humanity [5, 6]. The present energy resources, such as petrol, oil, natural gas, and mineral gases, are restricted and can be run out. Therefore, with the rapid increase in global energy demands, nuclear power has appeared as a viable solution to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions and increase energy intensity [7‚Äď9]. In the nuclear industry,
* Gulay Bayramoglu g_bayramoglu@hotmail.com
1 Biochemical Processing and Biomaterial Research Laboratory, Gazi University, 06500 Teknikokullar, Ankara, Turkey
2 Department of Chemistry, Sciences and Arts Faculty, Amasya University, 05100 Amasya, Turkey
3 Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Hacettepe University, 06800 Beytepe, Ankara, Turkey
4 Department of Environmental Science, Faculty of Engineering and Architecture, Ahi Evran University, 40100 KńĪrsehir, Turkey
5 Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Gazi University, 06500 Teknikokullar, Ankara, Turkey
U(VI) is a crucial element widely distributed in the Earth‚Äôs crust and especially abundant in seawater. The known reserves of uranium ore on land can be inadequate to meet the future demands of nuclear power plants. The estimated amounts of uranium in the seas are approximately 4.5 billion tons, around a thousand times more than the total land-based reserves [10, 11]. Therefore, selective and easy isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater is essential for the sustainable supply of uranium to nuclear energy resources [10‚Äď12]. Many methods have been reported for the isolation of U(VI) ions from seawater, such as chemical precipitation, photo-catalysis, membrane filtration, and adsorption [13‚Äď17]. Among these techniques, adsorption can be considered a sustainable method for U(VI) removal from seawater due to its low cost, large-scale applicability, high efficacy, and easy operation [18‚Äď22]. Numerous types of sorbents have been used for the adsorption of inorganic and organic from environment, including active carbon [23], composite materials [24, 25], cellulose [17, 26], chitosan [27, 28], metal‚Äďorganic framework [29], layered double zinc and aluminum hydroxide [30‚Äď32], hydrogels [33], various polymeric materials [34‚Äď36], biosorbents [24, 37], and magnetic adsorbents [38‚Äď41]. However, these prepared adsorbents have some advantages and disadvantages, and thus, to prepare reliable adsorbents with desired properties that are costeffective, easy to produce, highly durable, and possess a high
adsorption capacity under various experimental conditions. It should be noted that adsorbents containing amine groups are important for rapid adsorption because they easily interact with metal ions. Thus, they could effectively remove uranyl ions from seawater [7, 33, 38, 42‚Äď44]. In previous studies, researchers preferred to develop structures containing amine groups, especially polyamine and imidazole groups, when designing new adsorbents. For example, Bayramoglu et al. prepared agmatine ligand-modified magnetic alginate beads, and the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for U(VI) was ions 451.4 mg/g. Bayramoglu and Arica synthesized poly amidoxime group functionalized magnetic adsorbent and removed U(VI) ions from aqueous solutions. The maximum adsorption pH was observed at 6.0. Yang et al. [27] prepared amidoxime-modified chitosan adsorbent, uranium removal was studied from aqueous solution and seawater, and the maximum uranium adsorption capacities were 596 mg/g, and 243.2 őľg/g, respectively. Ivanov et al. (2024) prepared ¬≠Fe3O4 coated by Zn and Al layered double hydroxide for U(VI) removal, and the adsorption exchange capacity towards U(VI) ions was reported as 268.65 mg/g [32]. Additionally, adsorbents should be easily separable from the adsorption medium and not generate secondary pollutants. Magnetic materials can be simply removed from the solutions by using an external magnet. For this reason, magnetic adsorbents have received significant attention for removing metal ions from solutions [13, 32, 38]. A short time since nanotechnology has been applied in all branches of the sciences. With this understanding, new-generation adsorbents were prepared by grafting comb-type polymer chains in adsorption technology. In this concept, magnetic composite material can be grafted with a comb-type polymer to improve its adsorption performance using different functional polymers. Among the grafting methods, redox polymerization grafting is an easy polymerization method [35], which does not involve severe experimental conditions. In the grafting polymerization method, GMA is a widely used monomer, and its polymer has reactive epoxy groups. It can be utilized for coupling reactions with amine and thiol groups of the ligand molecules. Furthermore, the graft polymerization method generates fibrous polymer on the magnetic adsorbent, and various ligands can be attached to the fibrous polymer. This kind of adsorbent offers a high surface area [1]. In this study, magnetic chitosan beads were prepared via the phase inversion method and functionalized with hydrazone and imidazole groups. The synthesized adsorbents were characterized by FT-IR, SEM, BET, and analytical methods. The adsorption capacities of both adsorbents (i.e., Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads) for uranium under various experimental conditions were investigated in batch and continuous systems. The kinetic and thermodynamic models evaluated the adsorption mechanism between U(VI)
ions and the adsorbents. Finally, the performance of the assynthesized adsorbent was tested for adsorption of U(VI) ions from seawater obtained from the Black Sea and the Mediterranean Sea.
Materials and methods
Materials
Glycidyl methacrylate (GA), diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN, 98%), ethylenediamine, hydrazine hydroxylamine ¬≠(NH2OH), and chitosan (high molecular weight, deacetylation‚ÄČ‚Č•‚ÄČ75%), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Hamburg, Germany). U(VI) solution was prepared by dissolving uranyl acetate ¬≠(UO2(CH3COO)2¬∑2H2O) in deionized water and supplied from Honeywell Riedel-de-Haen AG (Germany). ¬≠FeCl3, ¬≠FeSO4¬∑7H2O, isopropanol (IPA), and all other chemicals were of analytical grade and purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).
Preparation of magnetic nanoparticles
The magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized via thermal co-precipitation reaction of Fe(III) and Fe(II) ions in a 2:1 molar ratio. For this purpose, ¬≠FeCl3 (6.5 g, 0.04 mol) and ¬≠FeSO4¬∑7H2O (5.6 g, 0.02 mol) were separately dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water. Then, the ¬≠FeCl3 (50 mL) and ¬≠FeSO4 (50 mL) solutions were transferred to a reaction vessel, and the temperature was raised to 45 ¬įC. After reaching the thermal equilibrium, ammonia solution (50 mL, 25% w/v) was added dropwise. The reaction temperature was set to 75 ¬įC and allowed to proceed for two hours under a nitrogen atmosphere. After this period, the reaction temperature was raised to 85 ¬įC and continued for one hour. After the reaction period, the magnetic nanoparticles ¬≠(Fe3O4) were separated using an external magnet and washed several times with distilled water to remove impurities. Then, they were dried in a vacuum oven under reduced pressures at 45 ¬įC.
Synthesis of magnetic chitosan beads
Magnetic chitosan beads were synthesized via the phase inversion method, as described previously [28]. Briefly, chitosan solution was prepared by adding 0.4 g of chitosan flake in 10% acetic acid solution (v/v, 40 mL). Then, 1.0 g of the above magnetic nanoparticles were added and magnetically mixed until a homogeneous solution was obtained. This mixture was dropped into NaOH solution (2.5 mol/L) using a syringe pump through an injection needle while continuously stirring the solution. The resulting ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT spheres were washed with distilled water and stored.
Grafting of magnetic chitosan beads with GMA and modification into hydrazone and imidazole groups
The magnetic chitosan beads were grafted with glycidylmethacrylate. The grafting reaction time was realized at different reaction times between 3.0 and 10 h to obtain the desired amount of GMA grafting density. Briefly, the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads (~‚ÄČ300 mg) were transferred to a reaction vessel, then isopropanol (20 mL) and deionized water (20 mL) were added and heated to 50 ¬įC under a nitrogen atmosphere. For the grafting reaction, GMA (2.0 mL) and KMnO‚āĄ (20 mL, 2.0 mmol/L) were added to this mixture, and the reaction was continued for 10 h. After this period, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads were washed with distilled water and ethanol 70% (v/v) to remove reaction impurities and then stored in ethanol (70%, v/v). Diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN) ligand was covalently attached to the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads via the reaction between the epoxy groups of p(GMA) and the amino groups of DAMN. Briefly, DAMN ligand (0.3 g) was dissolved in ethanol (100 mL, pH 9.0), and ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA) beads 0.5 g were added. The reaction mixture was rotated at 150 rpm and 60 ¬įC for 12 h. After the reaction, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN beads were washed with ethanol (70%, v/v) and distilled water and stored at room temperature. For hydrazone group modification, ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN beads (0.5) were transferred into a reaction medium containing hydrazine hydrate (15 mL) and ethanol (5 mL) at pH 8.0. The reaction medium was refluxed at 85 ¬įC for 6.0 h. At the end of the reaction period, the hydrazine-modified beads (i.e., ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD) were cleaned with ethanol and distilled water and then stored at room temperature. For imidazole group generation on the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD beads were reacted with ethylene diamine (EDA). An EDA solution (33%, 80 mL) containing 0.1 g of sulfur was prepared for this. Then, the solution temperature was raised to 90 ¬įC, and the dried ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA-HYD beads were added to the reaction medium. The reaction was continued at 95 ¬įC‚ÄČ¬Ī‚ÄČ5 ¬įC for 7.0 h. After the reaction period, the imidazole-modified beads ¬≠(Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ) were washed with deionized water at 50 ¬įC and then stored in ethanol: water mixture (70:30, v/v) at room temperature.
Characterization studies
The ATR-FTIR spectra of magnetic ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ, and beads were obtained using an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet iS5, Thermo Fisher Scientific, U.S.A.). The
surface morphology of the as-prepared magnetic beads was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI QUANTA 250 FEG, Oregon, USA). The magnetic properties of the ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, and ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA) beads were studied using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, 880; ADE Technologies, USA). The surface areas and average pore sizes of the ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads were obtained by the Brunauer‚ÄďEmmett‚ÄďTeller (BET) analysis. The ¬≠N2 adsorption/desorption experiments were studied using ASAP2020 HD88. The amount of epoxy groups on the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads was determined using the pyridine-HCl method described previously [1]. An ICP-MS spectrometer Thermo Fisher (iCAP TQe00061, Scientific, USA) with a plasma generator and the power output 1550 was used to determine metals. The as-prepared materials‚Äô x-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using Cu-KőĪ radiation, between 2őł of 20 and 80¬į, with a 0.1 increment and 2¬į ¬≠min‚ąí1 scan speed (MiniFlex 600, Rigaku).
Adsorption studies
The adsorption performances of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ were studied batch-wise. The adsorption experiments were conducted in a vial containing 20 mg of adsorbents and 30 mL of uranyl ions solution. The vials were placed on a shaker and rotated at 200 rpm and 25‚ÄČ¬Ī‚ÄČ2 ¬įC. The influence of pH on the adsorption performance was examined between pH 3.0 and 8.0, and U(VI) ions concentration was 200 mg/L. The effect of adsorbent amount on the adsorption performance was studied with different amounts of adsorbent in the range of 0.2‚Äď1.0 g/L with a uranium ions concentration of 200 mg/L. The impact of the initial concentration of uranium ions on adsorption capacity was investigated by varying the concentration of U(VI) ions between 1.0 and 400.0 mg/L. The effect of contact time on adsorption was investigated at a concentration of 400 mg/L of uranium ions for 180 min, and the sample was taken at predetermined time intervals. The adsorption performance of the adsorbents was also studied in synthetic seawater. One liter of synthetic seawater comprised of the following ingredients: NaCl: 24.53 g, MgCl‚āā: 5.20 g, CaCl‚āā:1.16 g, KCl: 0.695 g, ¬≠CuCl2: 20 mg, ¬≠NiCl2: 20 mg and ¬≠PbCl2 20 mg) and various amount of uranium ions was added to this solution between 0.5 and 10.0 mg/L. The amount of adsorbed U(VI) ions per unit of adsorbent [mg U(VI)/g adsorbent] was calculated by using the following equation:
(1) qe = (C0 ‚ąíCe‚ąēm) V
where ¬≠qe is the amount of U(VI) ion adsorbed onto the unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g), ¬≠C0 and ¬≠Ce are the concentrations
of the U(VI) ion before and after adsorption (mg/L), V is the volume of the solution (L), and m is the amount of the adsorbent (g).
Competing adsorption studies from binary mixture
The selectivity of the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads towards U(VI) ions (ionic radius: 138 pm) in the presence of competitive ions Cu(II) (ionic radius: 71 pm) and/or Ni(II) (ionic radius: 73 pm) was evaluated in a batch system. The metal ions solutions were prepared from Cu(NO3)2¬∑3H2O, Ni(NO3)2¬∑6H2O and Pb(NO3)2. A solution (20 mL) containing 1.0 mmol/L U(VI) ions and Cu(II) ions, Ni(II) or Pb(II) ions each of them 20 mmol/L was contacted with 1.0 g/L of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads at pH 6.0 and 25 ¬įC in the vial stirred at 200 rpm. After the adsorption period, the remaining concentration of each metal ion in the test solution was determined, as described above.
Removal of U(VI) ions from seawater in the continuous system
Removal of U(VI) ions from seawater in a continuous system was studied with Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads. The adsorbent was packed in a packed bed reactor (length: 14 cm; radius: 0.8 cm; volume: 28 mL), and the reactor was fed separately with the Black Sea water and the Mediterranean Sea water samples from the bottom inlet part at a flow rate of 100 mL/h with a peristaltic pump (model
IPC, Ismatec, D√ľsseldorf, Germany). The reactor was packed approximately with 3.8 g adsorbent and operated at room temperature (approximately 25 ¬įC) for 24 h.
Desorption studies
Adsorbed U(VI) ions were desorbed from the adsorbent by using 0.1 mol/L nitric acid solution. The Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were located in the desorption medium and stirred at 200 rpm at room temperature for 2.0 h. To determine the reusability of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads successive adsorption/ desorption rounds were repeated five times by using the same adsorbents. The desorption ratio was calculated from the amount of U(VI) ions released by the adsorbent and the final concentration of U(VI) ions in the adsorption medium.
Results and discussion
Characterization studies
A two-step process involved preparing ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads. ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads were prepared from magnetic nanoparticles and chitosan via the phase inversion method in the first step. The second step consisted of the graft polymerization of GMA monomer via redox polymerization in the presence of KMnO‚āĄ. The schematic representation of the preparation of adsorbents is presented in Fig. 1. The grafting
Fig.‚ÄĮ1‚Äā ‚ÄČSchematic representation of preparations of hydrazide and imidazole-modified adsorbents
reaction time was optimized to obtain high adsorption performance towards uranyl ions, and the polymerization time was realized up to 10 h. The amount of grafted p(GMA) on the magnetic chitosan beads was detected by the pyridineHCl method. The functional epoxy group content of the beads was found to be between 0.63 and 1.87 mmol/g beads. The ATR-FTIR spectra of ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles, CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA), ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD, and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMZD beads are presented in Fig. 2. The characteristic peak at 599 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 corresponded to the Fe‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČO stretching vibration of iron oxide nanoparticles. The presence of this peak confirmed the successful synthesis of ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 2A). The IR spectrum of chitosan exhibited characteristic peaks at 3354 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 due to‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČNH and ‚ÄďOH stretching vibrations. The peak at 1578 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 could have resulted from the ‚ÄďNH2 stretching vibration of chitosan (Fig. 2B). The FTIR spectra were also obtained to confirm the presence of ¬≠Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the chitosan beads at 599 ¬≠cm‚ąí1. The
Fig.‚ÄĮ2‚Äā ‚ÄČFT-IR Spectra of A ¬≠Fe3O4, B ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT, C ¬≠Fe3O4@CHTg-p(GMA), D ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-DAMN, E ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-gp(GMA)-HYD, and F ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT-g-p(GMA)-IMDZ
IR spectrum of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT displayed a band at 3347 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 due to‚ÄČ‚ąí‚ÄČNH or ‚ÄďOH stretching vibrations. Similar peaks (namely amide I, amid II, and Fe‚ÄďO were observed in this spectrum as in the case of pure chitosan. The FT-IR spectra of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads with characteristic peaks of epoxy and carbonyl groups at 901 cm‚ĀĽ1 and at 1717 cm‚ĀĽ1 were observed, respectively (Fig. 2C). The FTIR spectrum for ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-DAMN is presented in Fig. 2D. As observed from this spectrum, the band is due to antisymmetric stretching vibrations of the ¬≠NH2 groups observed at 3384 ¬≠cm‚ąí1. The characteristic stretching vibration peak of C‚Č°N was observed at 2360 ¬≠cm‚ąí1. The band at 1716 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 could be assigned for the ¬≠NH2 scissoring vibrations. The FT-IR spectra of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD beads showed peaks at around 3344 cm‚ĀĽ1 and 1640 cm‚ĀĽ1, corresponding to ‚ÄďNH‚āā stretching vibration and N‚ÄďH deformation, respectively, indicating the incorporation of amino groups from hydrazine into the structure of nitrile groups of DAMN on the beads (Fig. 3E). The FT-IR spectra of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ exhibited new bands at 3311 cm‚ĀĽ1 for N‚ÄďH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1528 and 1385 cm‚ĀĽ1 could be assigned for ‚ÄďNH2 and ‚ÄďCN stretching vibrations, confirming the formation of imidazole groups onto the HYD-modified beads after reaction with ethylene diamine (Fig. 2F). Furthermore, the new peak observed at 918 ¬≠cm‚ąí1 is related to the waging of the primary and secondary amines of the imidazole ring. Thus, as observed changes in the spectra, we conclude that the attached DAMN ligands were successfully converted to hydrazone and imidazole groups on the magnetic beads. The SEM image can provide information on the size, shape, and surface morphology of the as-prepared adsorbent material. The surface morphologies of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA) beads were studied using scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 3). As observed in Fig. 3A, a sphere-like structure was obtained for ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads, and at high magnification, the surface of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads was found to be a highly irregular structure (Fig. 3B). After grafting with p(GMA), a structural change in the surface morphology of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA) beads was observed and suggested that the grafting of the surface of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT beads with p(GMA) was successfully achieved (Fig. 3C). Effect of adsorbent dose on adsorption efficiency of U(VI) ions
The amount of adsorbent in the medium is a significant factor in pollutant removal studies. The adsorption of U(VI) onto Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ beads was studied at different amounts of adsorbent dose and changed between 0.2 and 1.0 g/L with an initial concentration of 200 mg/L. The adsorption efficiency of adsorbents for U(VI) under different adsorbent amounts is presented in Fig. 5. As seen in this figure, the amount of adsorbed U(VI) progressively increased with the increase of the adsorbent dosage. When the amount of adsorbent was 0.6 g/L, the removal percentage of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads reached 96.7% and 98.9%, respectively. Then, the removal percentage did not enhance meaningfully with increasing the adsorbent dosage. This could be because the available binding sites on the increasing amount of adsorbent could not be saturated with constant uranyl ions concentration in the solution. Thus, the amount of adsorbed U(VI) ions by each tested adsorbent was sensible with the residual amount of uranyl ions in the solution, causing a quiet adsorption percentage. As the adsorbent dosage was enhanced, the amount of adsorbed uranyl ions on the unit of the adsorbent dose gradually reduced due to a decrease in the amount of U(VI) ions in the unit adsorbent mass. For the best results in the remaining studies, 0.6 g/L of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were used in the U(VI) ions adsorption test.

The pH value of the aqueous solution is crucial in regulating the uranium species and the surface properties of the adsorbent, thereby influencing the uranium adsorption capacity [9]. Studies on the effect of pH on the adsorption of U(VI) ions onto Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were carried out in the pH range of 3.0‚Äď8.0. Moreover, control experiments without adsorbent were realized, and no precipitation of uranyl ions on the vial walls and any surface was observed at the studied pH range. The maximum uranyl ions adsorption on the Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads was observed between pH 6.0 and 7.0 (Fig. 6A). As observed from this figure, as the solution pH increased, the adsorption capacities of the tested adsorbents increased to pH 7.0 and then decreased. The protonation of hydrazone and imidazole groups of both adsorbents resulted in a positive surface charge on both adsorbents under acidic conditions. The pKa values of

At pH‚ÄČ‚Č§‚ÄČ5.0, uranyl ions species are positively charged predominantly in the form of ¬≠UO2 2+; thus, the electrostatic repulsion of both adsorbate and binding sites could prevent U(VI) ions from approaching the active binding center of the adsorbent, resulting in a decrease in the adsorption capacity of both adsorbent toward U(VI) ions. Therefore, the amount of adsorbed uranyl ions on the adsorbents can depend on the distribution of uranyl ion species at given pH values in the medium [46‚Äď49]. As a result, the adsorption capacity of ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads for uranyl ions were observed maximum at between pH 6.0 and 7.0. Due to the strong complexation of uranyl ions with the given above uranyl species, the main uranyl species were ¬≠UO2(CO3)2 2‚ąí and ¬≠UO2(CO3)3 4‚ąí at around pH 6.0 [1, 44]. Thus, the observed maximum adsorption capacities of uranyl ions on Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@
CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads between pH 6.0 and 7.0 could be explained by the affirmative coordination conditions between uranyl ionic species hydrazine and imidazole groups. The uranium (VI) speciation diagram is also presented in Fig. 6B. As can be seen from the figure, the significant forms of uranyl ions between pH 6.0 and 7.0 are ¬≠UO2CO3, ¬≠(UO2)CO3(OH)3 ‚ąí and ¬≠UO2(CO3)2 2‚ąí [44]. From these observations, the binding interaction of uranium ions on Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)HYD and Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads could be considered primarily as coordination complex formation, ion exchange interaction, and physical forces. It should be noted that Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ presented better adsorption capability for U(VI) ions than that of Fe‚āÉO‚āĄ@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD.

Regeneration and repeated use
For removing pollutants from wastewater, the ability to regenerate the adsorbent is a crucial economic consideration. Therefore, U(VI) ions laden ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were desorbed with a dilute nitric acid solution ¬≠(HNO3, 50 mM). The desorption series was repeated five times using the same magnetic beads. As can be seen in Fig. 12, ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads continued a stable adsorption ratio of approximately 95 and 91%, respectively, and the adsorption efficiencies of adsorbents for uranyl ions were merely decreased by about 5.0 and 9.0%.

Many continuous systems have been reported, such as stirred tank reactors, fluidized beds, and packed bed reactors. These continuous systems may exclude additional operational processes, such as filtration and centrifugation that require extra operational costs. Among these systems, the operation of a packed bed reactor may be easiest compared to another reactor system. The removal percentage of U(VI) in continuous systems using the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads was investigated. Determination of the adsorption of U(VI) in a continuous system can be important for understanding the dynamic behavior of the adsorption process. Therefore, the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were operated in a packed bed reactor and used to remove U(VI) ions from the Black Sea and Mediterranean Sea waters. The initial concentrations of U(VI) in the reactor feed solutions were 9.4 and 5.5 pbb for the Black Sea and Mediterranean Sea waters, respectively. It is essential to compare the adsorption efficiency of new adsorbents with present adsorbents for environmental applications. From Table 3, the adsorption capacities of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD and ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)IMDZ beads for the uranyl ions can be compared to the reported works in the literature [14, 31, 32, 37, 38, 54‚Äď57]. The observed high adsorption efficiencies of the as-prepared adsorbents can result from electrostatic and coordination complex interactions between the hydrazone and imidazole groups of each adsorbent and U(VI) ions. As presented above, the imidazole groups were more reasonable for removing U(VI) ions from the medium. The high adsorption performance of the imidazole groups for uranyl ions can be a result of the specific interaction of this functional group with uranyl ions.

Conclusion
In this study, magmatic chitosan beads were prepared via the phase inversion method and functionalized with diaminomaleonitrile ligand. The nitrile groups of the ligand were reacted with hydroxylamine and hydrazine hydrate, and the hydrozane and imidazole groups were obtained on the surface of the adsorbent and called ‚ÄúFe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads, respectively. The prepared adsorbents were characterized using FT-IR, SEM, VSM, and BET methods. The prepared ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were used for uranyl ions recovery from the aqueous medium, synthetic seawater in a batch system, and real seawater from the continuous system. The maximum adsorption capacities of the ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads were determined as 328.4 and 434.7 mg/g at pH 6.0, respectively. After five adsorption/desorption cycles, the uranium adsorption capacity was more than 87% of the initial adsorption capacity. The theoretical U(VI) ions adsorption capacity obtained from the Langmuir isotherm model was very near the experimental U(VI) ions adsorption capacity of the tested adsorbents. According to the tested kinetic models, the adsorption kinetics data of U(VI) ions on both adsorbents fitted the pseudo-second-order kinetics, which proposed that the chemisorption was the rate-limiting step. This research provides valuable insights into the potential of ¬≠Fe3O4@ CHT@p(GMA)-HYD or ¬≠Fe3O4@CHT@p(GMA)-IMDZ beads for effective removal of trace amount uranyl ions from synthetic seawater and real seawater.
